name: chana madra
notes:
Boil the chickpeas in turmeric first. When adding chickpeas to masala with yogurt / raisins / almonds, do not add more water, the recipe is hydrophobic. The almonds also need to be soaked for a few hours before adding. Note that I think ghee makes it worse. Porcini mushroom powder is non-indian but it helps a ton by adding depth, and lemon adds brightness. Cilantro is better than kasoori if available.

FENNEL AND CLOVES ARE KEY SPICES HERE: chana madra leans on warm, sweet, slightly anise-leaning spices more than the cumin/coriander base most other indian curries do. Fennel seeds and whole cloves carry a lot of the dish's signature character — without them it reads as a generic yogurt-chickpea curry rather than the himachali madra it actually is. Bloom both in oil at the tempering stage (along with bay leaf and any other whole spices). Cloves can be assertive, so don't go heavy — a few whole cloves are plenty.

ONION TREATMENT — COULD GO EITHER WAY: unlike chana masala and rajma (see chana-masala and rajma), chana madra doesn't have a clear winner between blended and coarse onions. Both work. Blended boiled onions balance the yogurt nicely (smooth-on-smooth), and that's a real argument for going that direction. But the recipe already has almonds, raisins, and peppers contributing chunky elements — this is more of a stew than a smooth gravy, and coarse onions don't read out of place. Pick whichever fits the texture you want on the day; both are defensible here.
